speaker: interviewer
text: You then came to this country with your father, and your mother remained behind to look for-- to be able to get her mother--

speaker: interviewee
text: Parents out.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Was she able to do it? Was she successful?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes they all-- all three of them went to Amsterdam to be with the rest of her family. But she had a Visa to go to England, and her parents insisted that she take advantage of it. And on the last boat before the war she went over to London. And she spent the war years and in London during the blitz, and saw-- [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: Your grandparents remained behind?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yeah. That whole family ended up in concentration camps--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: --eventually anyway. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: They did not. How did that connect with--

speaker: interviewee
text: How did-- how do we connect? All right. We connected as children already. And what triggered it just now was that your grandfather-- which I did not remember-- was in the import export of textiles. And I wouldn't be a bit surprised if that might have been the link of my father. Because he was in textiles in Hamburg with your grandparents. Yeah. And he was also trading in England and--

speaker: interviewee
text: But my grandparents were in Nuremberg.

speaker: interviewee
text: I know, but they came and visited. I see that this is a possibility, we don't really remember how her parents-- Ruth's parents-- and my parents met. That is a possibility. But I know that Ruth lived in the same neighborhood as we did. And my father was a pianist, and accompanied singers. And your mother was a singer, and was in the temple choir, as was my aunt Betty. So I was allowed to go up to the choir, and you were allowed to go up to the choir. We belonged to the same synagogue. So I would imagine that's how our parents met.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in Germany, even though we were not blood relations, I called her Tante [? Tilly. ?] And every-- they were very close friends in Hamburg already. As Ruth said, we would go on trips together every other weekend. We had a car. We had very happy family get togethers. And my parents with her parents had-- I remember Sylvester. What the heck is that? New Year's.

speaker: interviewee
text: New Year's.

speaker: interviewee
text: New Year's parties at our house. And we were allowed to look in. And we have some very happy times together. But what I remember personally, most, was her mother coming to England. And Ruth, you were already in New York with your father.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right

speaker: interviewee
text: So there was a separation there. But [? Tilly ?] worked in as a housekeeper. And on her take days off, she would come and visit. And she hated cats. And we had a cat. And she would, as soon as [? Tilly ?] would come, we would try to grab Topsy, [GERMAN]. Remove that animal. And always that cat came back. So we were very close. She was more my mother confessor than my mother. I was very close to Ruth's mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that now come to where you are? How did you re-meet, and how did that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well it was a number of years later. Well, why don't you--

speaker: interviewee
text: What year did your mother come from England? We saw her after she came to America--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right after the war. '45.

speaker: interviewee
text: --to see you, because-- in 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so your mother survived the war in England. And then in 1945--

speaker: interviewee
text: She came over here. My-- my father passed away a year after we came here to the States. He died. He had quite a bit of heart trouble. He had been beaten very severely during Crystal Night in Nuremberg. He had gone down to say goodbye to my maternal grandparents. And he got caught in Crystal Night, and ended up in the Jewish hospital. And when-- when they came to the hospital, they started throwing the patients out the windows. And he got up, and left, got on the first train home back to Hamburg. And he wasn't the same since then. He was a changed man when he arrived in Hamburg. He was all white, and he looked terrible. And I guess his heart trouble may have started then. I don't know. I'm not sure about that. But he had a very bad heart attack when he went from New York to St. Paul, where he was going to be living. And then he was in really bad shape for about a year, and passed away.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were left alone here, then.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I lived with my aunt and uncle-- my mother's brother in Denver. And they took me, and I stayed with them. And then I started on different jobs. I took care of a little boy for a couple of years. And then I went to business school, and got different jobs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you any news of your mother at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah, we were in touch. And, in fact, I had-- well Denver had an Air Force Base, and I had one boyfriend who was sent over to London. And he visited my mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: He visited my parens, because [? Tilly ?] was only a housekeeper, and didn't have anywhere to entertain, and she was a real lady. And she was only the housekeeper where she was staying. So she came to our place. And this Sam, in his American uniform, and [? Tilly ?] came to have coffee at our house. So there was a real relationship--

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't even know that.

speaker: interviewee
text: I met him! Sure, I met the Sam. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then, how did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: And then somehow we knew, I-- my uncle, I mentioned on-- on my tape, came to America. And then there were a lot of hamburgers in New York. And somehow the refugees knew what happened to the older generations-- you know, my parent's age, and Ruth's age. And my uncle and aunt knew that [? Tilly ?] had come to America, and knew that Ruth lived in-- on the West Coast. And probably had addresses.

speaker: interviewee
text: They kept in touch. They wrote. They wrote.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you first start seeing each other again, then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well you left in '39. I didn't-- I didn't even remember that. I left in December '38. And we came to America early 1960. And heard that my first husband, had a coronary. And we came to Los Angeles-- we were going to Los Angeles to the Pritikin Center. And then we remembered Ruth lived in Los Angeles, and this was in Santa Monica. And then we contacted each other. And [? Tilly, ?] Ruth's mother, and Leonard and Ruth came and ate that awful food-- [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: Terrible.

speaker: interviewee
text: --at the Pritikin Center. And we have been in touch.

speaker: interviewee
text: We seemed to come together then. And since then, we have stayed in touch.

speaker: interviewee
text: I came to Oxnard, to the Child Survival Conference in that year. I stayed some days here. And then my daughter Naomi moved to Los Angeles, and then I came. And here we are again. Always as a guest of Ruth, which is nice.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, come on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have a-- you have a chance to talk about the past and those--

speaker: interviewee
text: We do nothing but, seems like.

speaker: interviewee
text: My memory is-- I have a lot of detailed memories. I remembered a lot of incidents that Ruth can't recall.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have a complete blank about an awful lot of things in Germany. I don't remember names, or streets, or people, occasions. And Eva is a lot younger than I am.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember going to her school performances. She went to-- she is so much older, of course. So I looked up to Ruth, and she went to the high school, to a brand new school in-- in Hamburg [GERMAN]. And I remember being allowed to go and see that beautiful building, and I can smell it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when I went back to Germany in 1955 to 57, I had to go and open that front door. I remember the engraving on it. And I remember the door-- I think it was that, what is-- handle. I put my nose in that, and it smelled the same. It was a disinfectant or something.

speaker: interviewee
text: Smells trigger a lot of memories.

speaker: interviewee
text: Do for some people. That's how-- well I only was allowed to go there for a year and a half. Then they threw us out, the Jewish kids. There were two of us in the-- in the class. And we were thrown out. And from then on, we went to a Jewish school. And we had a interesting curriculum, because everybody who went there knew that eventually they were going to leave the country. And some of them knew they were going to go to Israel, so they learned Ivrit. And some of us knew we were-- Well, we always had English. They always taught English. We had two hours of English, every day, and one hour of French. So our history and geography was kind of fell by the wayside. But languages and math and so forth was strong. It was different than most schools.

speaker: interviewee
text: They had to provide for this coming and going.

speaker: interviewee
text: They did.

speaker: interviewee
text: You went to the [INAUDIBLE] class, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a coming and going. And it's funny. My mother, who couldn't sew, had to learn something practical. Because she didn't-- she knew she was probably going to England, and she wouldn't be able to be a teacher. And she'd have to earn her money. So they learned millinery, and your mother learned-- [? Paula ?] [? Zussman-- ?] we had common friends too-- learned how to make chocolates, because then you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't know that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They learned practical, professional stuff so that they would be able to utilize that without language in a foreign country, wherever they might get a visa.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well I think what was exceptionally nice for all these Hamburg people was that they saw each other in-- in London, and kept together, and visited. And it was a little bit of home to keep your old friendships, and-- and see your old friends. Which is different than a lot of people who had to go to Argentina, or someplace and didn't know a soul. And this-- the helped them get across with a lot of hardships. And they had each other to fall back on. I-- I think that was a big plus for-- There was a lot of helping each other, too. And not only-- also economically, they helped each other. That was a support group. There was no money, or finding of jobs, and they helped each other, eventually. And-- but the [INAUDIBLE]. Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is after-- you're talking about in England.

speaker: interviewee
text: In England. In England, yeah,

speaker: interviewer
text: I know this is a special time for you, because you are together. And I-- I hope you have an occasion-- many more occasions to be together again.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I hope so too.

speaker: interviewee
text: There's an attraction for us-- for us to come to the West Coast, and we--

speaker: interviewee
text: You have your daughter here.

speaker: interviewee
text: We have my daughter here, yes. But also the East Coast is not bad either, you know. I'd like to show you Philadelphia.

speaker: interviewer
text: I just wanted to thank you very much for sharing this moment with us, the two of you especially. I think it's very heartwarming to see you together and to know that you survived that experience, and then come out of it. And you're here together. I mean, that's very wonderful to see.

speaker: interviewee
text: We are happy to be here.

speaker: interviewer
text: We are going to-- I'm going to ask you to share some of your photographs, and some of your other memories with us. So again, thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my Moses Rosenbaum, who is my great-great grandfather. This was taken in 1908 in Mecklenburg, in Starnberg in Mecklenburg in Germany. And he was the patriarchal founder. Although our roots go back to Germany to 1350, we do not have pictures. He is the oldest member of our family we have a picture of. 1899, this is my mother's family. Her great grandfather, who was then 90. Next to the old gentleman is my Aunt Frieda. And next to that, the little one is my mother. Behind her is Reicha Jakobus. The family was called Jakobus. And they lived in Berlin. But they came from-- Schiefelbein and Nuremberg originally. And I never knew, of course my great grandfather, or my maternal grandmother. My Aunt Frieda I knew well. They lived in Berlin. Here's a family picture. And the reason I like it, and I chose this one is my mother always boasted that her hair was long enough to sit on. There she is aged 10. Now sitting next to her grandfather with her parents behind her in Berlin, and the sisters on each side. But that was the age when I left Germany, and that's why she always compared it. Look, my hair was so much longer than yours when I was 10. This is Louis Rosenbaum in Starnberg, Mecklenburg. My grandfather, he had a department store, and was a very likable, ordinary man.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know about when this was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1908 I think. I'm not quite certain.

speaker: interviewer
text: Beautiful.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my only grandmother I knew, Augusta Rosenbaum [? geborne ?] [? Lichtman. ?] She was born in [NON-ENGLISH], and went to Starnberg, Mecklenburg to get married. And she hated being there. She was a musician. She was somebody who loved the theatre. She had a lot of energy. And there she was in this little village, and hating it. So every opportunity she had, she would walk to the station and go to the nearest big city to the opera, or what have you. First school year, 1936 in Hamburg. And the first and last one. I only went to public school one year.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes I'm in there. I'm on the-- in the middle row, the third one from the front. [POINTING] Correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: I just say--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm-- on this picture, this is the school I went to, the Jewish school in Hamburg, 1937. In 1947 in London, my parents and my brother-- I had just graduated from seminary, and it was a happy get together in our garden in London. This is my German passport showing the swastika.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is my passport, 1938, before leaving Germany for England. One second. One second.

speaker: interviewer
text: I had said that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my daughter, Naomi Alexander, on that day, born Abraham. And my son on the right, Daniel Abraham. This is the sample book that we are hoping to publish called which another [? kind ?] and I have put together. They're taken-- there's true stories taken from testimonies I obtained on oral tapes for Gratz College in Philadelphia. And I did over 20 interviews with kinder who went from kinder transport from Austria, Germany, or Czechoslovakia to England. There were 10,000. And these are some stories written up for the younger readers.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is Ruth Chernoff's family. Ruth appeared on the tape with Eva as the friend-- the friend from Hamburg.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is my father Ivan [? Heinler ?], and myself in the center and my mother. [? Tilly ?] [? Heinler ?], and it was taken, oh, approximately 1936 at our home in Hamburg.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I-- I believe this must have been before 1937, when-- there's Eva on the left, and her brother Peter, and myself on the right. 1934 looks more like it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: On one of our outings, I think. [LAUGHS] On a Sunday when we went on an outing. This is our new reform temple in Hamburg, which was a beautiful building. And my father's father was the cantor there. My mother sang in the choir, as did Eva's aunt. And we spent a lot of time there. It was damaged during Crystal Night. Yeah. This is my passport picture which was taken January 1939 before we left, the end of January in 1939 for America.
